title: Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks
summary: This paper combines a parametric sequence model with a non-parametric document retriever so the model can condition generation on retrieved evidence instead of relying only on memorized knowledge.
methods: Use dense retrieval to fetch supporting passages at generation time.
Fuse retrieved documents with a generator to answer knowledge-intensive questions.
findings: Retrieval grounding improves factuality and adaptability on open-domain QA and related knowledge tasks.
External memory makes it easier to update knowledge without retraining the whole model.
limitations: System quality still depends heavily on retriever recall and evidence coverage.
The paper focuses on retrieval augmentation rather than complex multi-step tool use.

title: ReAct: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting in Language Models
summary: ReAct alternates reasoning traces with actions so a language model can plan, gather observations, and revise its next step based on tool feedback.
methods: Interleave chain-of-thought style reasoning with explicit actions.
Use observations from external environments to steer later reasoning steps.
findings: Reasoning plus action helps with tasks that require both deliberation and external information gathering.
The prompting pattern is more interpretable than hidden-only reasoning because intermediate plans and actions are visible.
limitations: Performance is prompt-sensitive and can degrade when the model fails to choose useful actions.
The paper demonstrates the pattern but does not solve robust long-horizon memory management.

title: Toolformer: Language Models Can Teach Themselves to Use Tools
summary: Toolformer trains a language model to decide when and how to call external tools by inserting API calls into text and learning from the resulting improvements.
methods: Generate synthetic demonstrations of API usage from a small seed set.
Train the language model to invoke tools such as calculators, search, or translation APIs.
findings: A model can learn tool invocation behavior from self-supervised data generation rather than full manual annotation.
Properly chosen API calls improve factuality and task coverage on tool-relevant queries.
limitations: The approach depends on a curated tool set and a filtering process for useful synthetic calls.
It focuses on single-step API use more than rich multi-agent orchestration.

title: Self-RAG: Learning to Retrieve, Generate, and Critique through Self-Reflection
summary: Self-RAG extends retrieval-augmented generation with learned reflection behavior so the model can decide when to retrieve, how to critique its own output, and when evidence is sufficient.
methods: Train the model with special reflection tokens that control retrieval and critique behavior.
Let the generator judge whether more evidence is needed before continuing.
findings: Joint retrieval and self-critique improves answer quality and evidence use compared with static retrieval pipelines.
The model can avoid unnecessary retrieval on easier questions while still grounding harder cases.
limitations: Training requires specially constructed supervision for reflection decisions.
The method still inherits weaknesses from the underlying retriever and base model.

title: AutoGen: Enabling Next-Gen LLM Applications via Multi-Agent Conversation
summary: AutoGen presents a conversation-centric framework in which specialized agents and optional human users collaborate through structured message passing to solve tasks.
methods: Represent workflows as conversations among assistant agents, user proxies, and tools.
Allow code execution, tool invocation, and human feedback inside the same interaction loop.
findings: Multi-agent conversation can make complex tasks easier to decompose into specialized roles.
The framework lowers prototyping cost for agentic systems that need iterative coordination.
limitations: Conversation-heavy workflows can become expensive and unstable without strong control policies.
The framework itself does not guarantee correctness or efficient planning.

title: MetaGPT: Meta Programming for Multi-Agent Collaborative Framework
summary: MetaGPT models software production as a company-like workflow where role-specific agents follow structured operating procedures to produce design, code, and review artifacts.
methods: Assign roles such as product manager, architect, engineer, and reviewer to different agents.
Use standard operating procedures to constrain communication and artifact handoff.
findings: Role specialization can improve coordination compared with unconstrained agent discussion.
Structured intermediate artifacts help keep long workflows aligned with product requirements.
limitations: The framework targets software workflows and may not transfer cleanly to every domain.
Complex multi-agent pipelines still face compounding error and cost issues.

title: Voyager: An Open-Ended Embodied Agent with Large Language Models
summary: Voyager uses an LLM-driven embodied agent in Minecraft that builds a reusable skill library and an automatic curriculum to keep exploring harder tasks over time.
methods: Generate a curriculum of tasks automatically as the agent progresses.
Store executable skills in a library that can be retrieved and reused later.
findings: Skill accumulation and curriculum design help the agent explore more effectively than one-shot prompting.
The environment demonstrates how memory and iterative execution matter for long-horizon tasks.
limitations: Results are tied to a sandboxed game environment and do not directly solve general software tasks.
Success depends on reliable code execution and environment feedback.

title: SWE-agent: Agent-Computer Interfaces Enable Automated Software Engineering
summary: SWE-agent studies how an agent-computer interface can improve language-model performance on software issue resolution by structuring file navigation, editing, and test execution.
methods: Design an interface that exposes codebase navigation and editing actions to the agent.
Combine repository interaction with evaluation on realistic software engineering tasks.
findings: Better interfaces materially improve agent performance on bug-fixing benchmarks.
Task success depends on both language-model reasoning and environment affordances.
limitations: The benchmark is expensive and sensitive to execution environment details.
The paper is about software engineering agents rather than scientific document QA.
